title: Wholemeal scones
subtitle: Nutty, hearty scones with a tender crumb
excerpt: Scones made with a mix of wholemeal and plain flour, giving a nuttier flavour and more substantial crumb than an all-white scone. Good with both sweet and savoury toppings.
sourceNotes: Wholemeal scones are a British baking tradition documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, and in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, both of which record brown flour scone recipes. The use of a mix of wholemeal and white flour for a lighter crumb than all-wholemeal is standard modern British practice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones. Reheat from frozen at 180°C fan for 8–10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Scones are best eaten on the day. The dry ingredients can be measured and kept in a bowl, with butter rubbed in, ready to mix with liquid just before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rubbed-in mixture of flour, fat, and leavening that forms the base of a scone dough. The fat is incorporated cold and quickly to keep the mixture crumbly and light.

content.text: A half-wholemeal, half-plain flour blend gives scones a noticeably nuttier flavour and slightly denser texture than all-white. The bran in the wholemeal flour shortens the gluten strands and gives the scone a more rustic, open crumb. These are excellent with mature cheese, or with jam and clotted cream where the wholemeal flavour works with the richness rather than against it.
The rules for a good scone apply regardless of flour type: cold fat, minimal mixing, a hot oven, and eating them fresh. The
can be made a few hours ahead up to the point of adding liquid; from there, mix, shape, and bake quickly.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan (240°C conventional) and line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine {{wholemeal-bread-flour}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} and rub in quickly until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs with some pea-sized pieces remaining. Pour in {{whole-milk}} and mix briefly with a fork until the dough just comes together; it will be sticky and rough. Do not overwork.

Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat to 2.5 cm thickness without rolling — rolling develops the gluten and toughens the scones. Cut into 6 cm rounds with a straight-sided cutter or a sharp knife. Place on the prepared tray close together for softer sides or spaced apart for crustier ones. Brush the tops with beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 12–15 minutes until well risen and deep brown on top; the base should sound hollow when tapped. Transfer to a cooling rack and eat warm.
All-wholemeal scones are denser but more flavourful. Reduce the milk by 10 ml as wholemeal absorbs more liquid.
Twist-cutting with a round cutter seals the sides and prevents a good rise. Press and lift straight down.
For savoury wholemeal scones: omit the sugar and fold in 75 g of grated strong cheddar and a pinch of mustard powder.
